coredata.dc:title: Heteroatom-free conjugated tetraphenylethylene polymers for selectively fluorescent detection of tetracycline
coredata.dc:creator: Zhuang, Qiu
Zhang, Cheng
Zhuang, Haiyan
Deng, Hanyu
Lin, Xiangpeng
Li, Yan
Chen, Hao
Xie, Aming
Dong, Wei
coredata.dc:description: The antibiotic tetracycline (Tc) is a major contaminant in food and water, with adverse effects on both ecosystems and human health. The development of novel sensors for tetracycline detection is of great importance. In this work, we develop a novel heteroatom-free conjugated tetraphenylethylene polymer (TPE-CMP) fluorescence sensor for the detection of tetracycline. In the presence of Tc, the emission fluorescence of TPE-CMP was quenched by the photoinduced electron transfer mechanism to achieve high sensitivity. The polymers can detect tetracycline at a concentration of 0–100 μg/mL with a good linear correlation (0.99), and the limit of detection (LOD) is 1.23 μg/mL. Furthermore, TPE-CMP has excellent selectivity in detecting Tc in the presence of various anti-interference analytes, including ions and antibiotics. In addition, the practical feasibilities of TPE-CMP for Tc sensing were further investigated in milk, urine and wastewater samples with satisfactory recoveries (from 94.96% to 112.53% for milk, from 96.41% to 99.31% for urine and from 98.54% to 100.52% for wastewater). We have designed and synthesized TPE-CMP based on heteroatom-free for the specific fluorescence detection of tetracycline, expanding the range of fluorescence detection sensors and offering great promise for practical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Heteroatom-free
Tetraphenylethylene
Conjugated microporous polymers
Tetracycline
Fluorescent detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Sensing and selectivity of TPE-CMP for Tc detection
The mechanism for detection of Tc

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The work was financially supported by the Jiangsu Provincial Natural Science Foundation, China (No. BK20180453 ), National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 11802134 ), and China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No. 2018M642255 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a)The 13C CP/MAS NMR spectrum of TPE-CMP, Signals with ∗ symbols are side peaks. (b) FT-IR spectra of monomers (BDC and TPE) and polymer (TPE-CMP).
Images of TPE-CMP: SEM for (a, b), TEM for (c, d).

(a) Powder X-ray diffraction profiles of CMP-TPE and the recovered TPE-CMP after reaction with Tc (TPE-CMP-1). (b) thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) curves of TPE-CMP with a 10 °C/min heat rate surrounded by nitrogen. (c) Nitrogen adsorption and desorption isotherm of TPE-CMP and the recovered TPE-CMP after reaction with Tc (TPE-CMP-1) measured at 77.3 K. (d) Pore size distribution of TPE-CMP and the recovered TPE-CMP after reaction with Tc (TPE-CMP-1).

TPE-CMP in water (0.04 mg/mL), Concentration of Tc (100 μg/mL): Fluorescence excitation spectra of TPE-CMP in water before (a), and after (b) addition of Tc; Fluorescence emission spectra of TPE-CMP in water before (c), and after (d) addition of Tc. inset: The picture compared before (left) and after (right) adding Tc under the UV lamp (365 nm).
(a) Fluorescence spectra of TPE-CMP (0.04 mg/mL) with increasing concentration of Tc (0–100 μg/mL) in water. (b) curves of fluorescence intensity versus the concentrations of Tc.
The selectivity of the TPE-CMP to various ion. TPE-CMP:0.04 μg/mL, Tc antibiotics:100 μg/mL, ion concentration:1.0Ⅹ10−3 M, solvent (water).
The selectivity of the TPE-CMP to various antibiotics. TPE-CMP:0.04 μg/mL, antibiotics:100 μg/mL, solvent (water).
The mechanism of TPE-CMP FL quenching after addition of Tc.
Experiment design for Tc detection using heteroatom-free conjugated tetraphenylethylene polymers (TPE-CMP).
Results of detecting Tc in milk sample.
Results of detecting Tc in urine samples.
Results of detecting Tc in wastewater samples.

head.ce:title: Heteroatom-free conjugated tetraphenylethylene polymers for selectively fluorescent detection of tetracycline

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Nanjing University of Science & Technology, Nanjing, 210094, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering, Nanjing University of Science & Technology, Nanjing, 210094, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory for Marine Corrosion and Protection, Luoyang Ship Material Research Institute (LSMRI), Xiamen, 361100, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory for Marine Corrosion and Protection

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory for Marine Corrosion and Protection, Luoyang Ship Material Research Institute (LSMRI), Xiamen, 361100, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Mechanical Engineering, Nanjing University of Science & Technology, Nanjing, 210094, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Mechanical Engineering, Nanjing University of Science & Technology, Nanjing, 210094, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The antibiotic tetracycline (Tc) is a major contaminant in food and water, with adverse effects on both ecosystems and human health. The development of novel sensors for tetracycline detection is of great importance. In this work, we develop a novel heteroatom-free conjugated tetraphenylethylene polymer (TPE-CMP) fluorescence sensor for the detection of tetracycline. In the presence of Tc, the emission fluorescence of TPE-CMP was quenched by the photoinduced electron transfer mechanism to achieve high sensitivity. The polymers can detect tetracycline at a concentration of 0–100 μg/mL with a good linear correlation (0.99), and the limit of detection (LOD) is 1.23 μg/mL. Furthermore, TPE-CMP has excellent selectivity in detecting Tc in the presence of various anti-interference analytes, including ions and antibiotics. In addition, the practical feasibilities of TPE-CMP for Tc sensing were further investigated in milk, urine and wastewater samples with satisfactory recoveries (from 94.96% to 112.53% for milk, from 96.41% to 99.31% for urine and from 98.54% to 100.52% for wastewater). We have designed and synthesized TPE-CMP based on heteroatom-free for the specific fluorescence detection of tetracycline, expanding the range of fluorescence detection sensors and offering great promise for practical applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Antibiotics are widely used in the prevention and infection of microbial diseases because of their antibacterial effect and low cost [1]. On the one hand, environmental pollution caused by the abuse of antibiotics has become a serious public problem. On the other hand, owing to its insufficient metabolism, it has become a serious public problem that seriously threatens human health [2,3]. Tetracycline (Tc) antibiotics are usually employed to treat animal and human infections, because of its broad-spectrum antibacterial activity, relatively low cost and toxicity, and good oral absorption [4–7]. In addition, tetracycline antibiotics can promote the growth of animals, so they are used as feed additives in animal husbandry [8]. However, the excessive use of Tc in animal husbandry and aquaculture can lead to residues in animal products including milk and meat [9]. Tc residues in food may cause serious harm to humans through the food chain, such as allergic reactions, inhibition of bone growth, hearing loss, and kidney toxicity [10,11]. Meanwhile, the excessive residues of Tc in the environment can also cause bacterial resistance to antibiotics [12]. Therefore, many researchers are devoted to developing more simple, rapid and reliable methods for determining antibiotic residues for the well-being of human and environmental safety.

Many studies have reported various methods for Tc detection, including high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [5,13], capillary electrophoresis (CE) [14,15], mass spectrometry [16], microbiological analysis (MA) [17], Raman spectroscopy (RS) [18], photoelectrochemical analysis [19], and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay [20]. In recent years, most of those methods have been successfully used to detect Tc with high accuracy and sensitivity. However, these analytical tools also have their inherent shortcomings, like requiring expensive and sophisticated instruments, either time-consuming or complicated sample preparation, thus restraining their further application in the routine analysis [21]. Therefore, developing a reliable and cost-effective method for rapid and sensitive detection of antibiotic residues is essential to human health and preventing the unrestricted spread of antibiotic-resistant bacteria. As an effective method for detection of tetracycline, fluorescence has attracted much attention in the field of pollution monitoring due to its convenient operability, high sensitivity, fast response time, ease of visual analysis and eco-friendliness [22–24]. The key to antibiotic detection technology, which is a prerequisite for antibiotic therapy, is constructing specific fluorescent probes.

Recently, many researchers have reported the fluorescence sensors in detecting tetracycline. Many materials are involved, such as metal-organic framework (MOF) [25–32], composite materials based on lanthanide metals [33,34]. silicon modified carbon dots [35,36], boron-doped carbon dots [37], nitrogen-doped carbon dots and so on [38]. Among them, the MOF materials contain metal elements, which may cause secondary pollution. Carbon dots based on various elemental modifications are difficult to explore in relation to their structure and properties, which prevents their further understanding and application. It is also worth noting that most of these systems have problems such as low sensitivity, complex synthesis procedure and short signal response time. Those have aroused people's attention and provided an opportunity to develop new materials and methods for detecting Tc in water and other competitive environments. This will prevent the threat of the lasting spread of antibiotic-resistant bacteria.

In the field of polymer or small molecule fluorescence detection of tetracycline, some reports have aroused our interest. For example, oxygen-containing conjugated compounds have been reported for the detection of tetracycline [39], and polymers containing silicon and oxygen have been reported for the detection of tetracycline [40], non-conjugated polymers containing nitrogen and oxygen were used for the fluorescence detection of tetracycline [41], and the conjugated microporous polymers with pyridine unit have been reported to be used for the fluorescence detection of tetracycline [42]. To our best knowledge, no work has been reported and realized on the fluorescence detection of tetracycline based on polymers without heteroatom doping, which has aroused our great interest. We found tetraphenylethylene has been widely explored as an aggregation-induced emission molecule since its discovery [43]. We use it as a monomer to synthesize heteroatom-free conjugated tetraphenylethylene polymers with fluorescent properties.

In this work, we reported the design of heteroatom-free conjugated tetraphenylethylene polymers (TPE-CMP), combing the monomers of tetrakis(4-bromophenyl)ethene (TPE) and benzene-1,4-diboronic acid (BDC) via palladium-catalyzed Suzuki Cross-Coupling polymerization.

To our knowledge, this is the first time that polymer material based solely on carbon and hydrogen elements has been used for the fluorescence detection of tetracycline. It erases the effects of other factors such as secondary contamination during Tc detection, such as metal leakage problems. The structure and fluorescence properties of TPE-CMP were confirmed by a series of characterizations. This method had a wide detection range of 0–100 μg/mL with a low limit of detection (LOD) 1.23 μg/mL. In addition, TPE-CMP has been developed to selectively detect Tc in the presence of various anti-interference analytes that may compete and interfere with its detection process. TPE-CMP has also shown good recoveries in the detection of milk and urine sample. We have demonstrated that TPE-CMP has a high potential for practical applications.

Tetrakis(4-bromophenyl)ethene was purchased from Hines Biochemical Ltd., Tianjin, China. N, N-Dimethylformamide (DMF) was purchased from Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Co., LTD, Shanghai, China. Benzene-1,4-diboronic acid, Tetrakis(triphenylphosphine)palladium (Pd(PPh3)4) were purchased from Energy Chemical Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China. Tetracycline (Tc), Erythromycin (Ery), Trimethoprim (TMP), Levofloxacin hydrochloride (LVFX), Streptomycin sulfate (SS), kanamycin sulfate (KS), Vancomycin hydrochloride (VCH) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Co., Ltd. All purchased reagents and chemicals were used directly without further purification.

The prepared TPE-CMP structure analysis was conducted on SSNMR-Solid Nuclear Magnetic Resonance Spectrometer (400 MHz) on AVANCE III WB 400 MHz instrument. The surface morphologies of parent TPE-CMP were characterized by a field scanning electron microscope of Quanta FEG 250 (SEM). Transmission electron microscopy of TECNAI G2 20 LaB6 (TEM) was used for collecting images of TPE-CMP. The micro-structures of TPE-CMP analyzed by powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) of D8ADVANCE with Cu-Kα (λ = 1.5406 Å) radiation with a scanning range of 10–80° (2θ) at a scanning rate of 20 deg/min. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was used to identify the chemical bonds of TPE-CMP on a NICOLETIS10 instrument in the 400–4000 cm−1 range with the KBr pellet technique. The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) and Density functional theory (DFT) methods were used to calculate the surface areas, total pore volume, and pore size distribution from nitrogen adsorption and desorption isotherms that were obtained with the ASAP 3020 Physisorption system, Micromeritics. Fluorescent spectra were obtained using a Shimadzu RF5301 fluorescence spectrophotometer. UV–vis spectra were measured using a Shimadzu UV-2550 spectrometer. pH values were carried out with a Sartorius (PB-10) digital pH meter (Lei Ci, Shanghai, China). The optical properties of TPE-CMP were studied by UV–vis adsorption spectra and fluorescence spectra at room temperature. The particle size of TPE-CMP in solution was measured by Dynamic Light Scattering (Nano-ZS90).

K2CO3 (2.0 M, 5 mL) and Pd(PPh3)4 (0.015 g, 0.012 mmol) were added to a mixture of Tetrakis(4-bromophenyl)ethene (0.648 g, 1.0 mmol), Benzene-1,4-diboronic acid (BDC) (0.332g, 2.0 mmol) and N,N-Dimethylformamide (30 mL). The mixture was degassed and purged with argon (Ar) through three freeze-pump-thaw cycles. The treated sample was gradually heated up to 150 °C and kept for 48 h under Ar atmosphere. The entire heating process is carried out under stirring. After the reaction was completed, the reaction solution was cooled to room temperature naturally, and then filtered. The residue was washed with water, methanol and methylene chloride sequentially. After drying out, there is about 610 mg of gray-green powdery product.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing and selectivity of TPE-CMP for Tc detection
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence measurement is performed by a fluorescence spectrometer with a quartz cell. 4 mg of TPE-CMP powder was fully dispersed a test tube filled with 40 mL of water by sonicating for about 10 min 200 μL TPE-CMP solution was taken out, then various amounts of Tc solution (1 mg/mL) were added, afterward adding water to dilute to 500 μL for luminescence detection experiment. The emission spectra of these suspensions were collected. For TPE-CMP solution, the excitation wavelength for luminescence measurement is 425 nm. The selectivity experiments were performed similarly by using other antibiotics.

Currently, depending on the complexity of the actual environment, the detection of Tc is inevitably interfered by other species. The TPE-CMP needs to meet the selective requirement for Tc identification. The detection of Tc by TPE-CMP was performed in the presence of other metal cations (Ba2+, Bi3+, Ca2+, Cr6+, Cu2+, K+, Na+, Ni2+, Pb2+) and anions (S2O8 2−, S2O5 2−, SO4 2−, CO3 2−, NO3 −, HCO3 −). The ion concentration is:1.0Ⅹ10−3 M. We also chose other antibiotics (Ery, TMP, LVFX, SS, KS, VCH) to study the selectivity of TPE-CMP. The dosage of other antibiotics is 100 μg/mL.

The detection of antibiotics, contaminants and additives in food is receiving more and more attention because they greatly affect people's health [44–46]. We chose milk as the food sample for testing, which was purchased from local supermarkets, to investigate the application of our strategy. The sample was treated before accurate analysis, Firstly, 10 mL 2% Trichloroacetic acid was added to 2.5 g fresh milk to remove the complex proteins. After 5 min of centrifugation (10000 rpm), the transparent supernatant was adjusted to pH = 7 with NaOH and taken out for further analysis. The urine sample was collected from our local public toilet, 10 mL of clear liquid was collected, filtered with a 0.22 μm filter membrane, and then centrifuged at 10000 rpm in the laboratory for 30 min, and the supernatant was diluted to 10% for further analysis. To study the application of this sensor in environmental samples, we took wastewater samples from a local river. The wastewater samples were filtered and centrifuged, and the supernatant was used for further analysis.

The strategy of the designed heteroatom-free polymer sensor for fluorescence detection of Tc is shown in Scheme 1 . First, we polymerized Tetrakis(4-bromophenyl)ethene and p-diphenylboronic acid to form heteroatom-free conjugated tetraphenylethylene polymers (TPE-CMP) through Suzuki Coupling. The TPE-CMP sensor emits 535 nm light under the excitation of 425 nm wavelength, The fluorescence of TPE-CMP solution can be effectively quenched by addition of Tc.

The solid-state 13C CP/MAS NMR spectroscopy (Fig. 1 a) was measured to confirm the chemical structure of TPE-CMP. It can be seen there are two broad peaks approximately at 138.7 and 125.6 ppm, corresponding to the substituted vinyl and phenyl carbons (138.7 ppm) and the unsubstituted phenyl carbons (125.6 ppm), respectively [47–49]. To further demonstrate our successful synthesis of TPE-CMP, we measured the FTIR spectra of TPE-CMP and compared them with the building monoliths used as reference. (Fig. 1b). A direct evidence for the occurrence of Suzuki condensation is the absence of the 3200-3450 cm−1 band belonging to BDC in the FTIR spectrum of TPE-CMP, which is attributed to the vibration of the hydroxyl group. Comparing the FTIR spectra of the polymer and other monomers, the TPE-CMP becomes sharp around 3030 cm-1 (attributed to the stretching vibration of the hydrocarbon on the benzene ring), which corresponds to the polymerization of the BDC and TPE monomers. Another conclusive evidence is that the peak at 1072 cm−1 belongs to the C-Br vibration of the monomer (TPE), which disappears after the Suzuki Coupling reaction, as can be seen in the TPE-CMP sample [50]. The peak at 1580 cm−1 (C=C) in the FTIR spectrum for TPE monomer appears in the TPE-CMP spectrum. The peaks at 1670 cm−1 (C=C) belongs to TPE-CMP become sharp compared with the other monomers. The peaks at 750-890 cm−1 belong to the out-of-plane C-H bending absorption of benzene. These results indicated that the BDC and TPE have been well polymerized to form TPE-CMP. In addition, we examined the recovered TPE-CMP after detecting Tc (TPE-CMP-1). Combining the FTIR profiles of Tc, TPE-CMP and TPE-CMP-1. TPE-CMP-1 is essentially identical to TPE-CMP and no peak for Tc was found in the FT-IR spectra of TPE-CMP-1 (Fig. S1). These data indicate that Tc is difficult to accumulate on the surface of TPE-CMP. This is consistent with the results of the zeta potential tests (Fig. S2).

The morphology of the prepared TPE-CMP was observed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Fig. 2 a shows the TPE-CMP is formed by aggregating a number of small irregular spheres. These irregular spheres are interconnected to form an approximate cylinder. (Fig. 2b). The TEM images show that the diameter of the pellets that make up TPE-CMP is around 300 nm (Fig. 2c and d). These nanospheres are clustered together in an irregular fashion. Powder X-ray diffraction of TPE-CMP shows that it is an amorphous structure with no apparent crystal structure (Fig. 3 a). In addition, we investigated the structure of the recovered TPE-CMP after detecting Tc (TPE-CMP-1) and found that its structure remained unchanged and amorphous. Moreover, in the small angle range (2–20°), no significant peaks were observed for both materials as well (Fig. S3).

The thermal stability of TPE-CMP was characterized by Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) experiment under N2 atmosphere (Fig. 3b). As can be seen, TPE-CMP maintains a weight of over 96% up to 400 °C, so it exhibits good thermal stability. The excellent thermal stability ensures that TPE-CMP retains its properties even in extreme environments. The porous structure of TPE-CMP networks was investigated by N2 gas sorption analysis at 77 K. Fig. 3c shows the N2 adsorption and desorption isotherms of the TPE-CMP and TPE-CMP-1. The Bruker-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area and t-Plot micropore volumes for TPE-CMP and TPE-CMP-1 are 294.0037 m2 g−1 and 0.122345 cm3 g−1, 251.2801 m2 g−1 and 0.045476 cm3 g−1 respectively (Fig. 3c). It can be seen that TPE-CMP and TPE-CMP-1 shows type I characteristics, corresponding to high absorption at low pressure (P/P0 = 0.1), indicating a predominance of micropores (<2 nm) [51–54]. The result is further supported by the analysis of the pore size distribution curves (DFT method) (Fig. 3d). It can be clearly seen that the pore size of TPE-CMP is concentrated at 0.80419 nm and 1.17948 nm, while the pore size of TPE-CMP-1 is significantly larger than that of TPE-CMP, concentrated at 1.48329 nm. The reduction in specific surface area and the increase in pore size may stem from the fact that some of the tetracycline is adsorbed into the micropores of TPE-CMP, where it is difficult to detect by FT-IR, making it difficult to detect the characteristic infrared peak for Tc in TPE-CMP-1 (Fig. S1). We also investigated the particle size distribution of TPE-CMP during the detection of tetracycline by Dynamic Light Scattering. After the addition of Tc, the particle size of TPE-CMP in aqueous solution did not change significantly, increasing from approximately 24.1 μm to approximately 27.4 μm in diameter (Fig. S4). The Dv50 of the TPE-CMP and TPE-CMP-1 aqueous solution was 30.9 μm and 31.9 μm respectively. The TPE-CMP particles in aqueous solution showed no significant particle aggregation or dispersion during the Tc detection.

Some experiments have been carried out to investigate the optical properties of TPE-CMP. We investigated the solubility of TPE-CMP in aqueous solvents. As can be seen from Fig. S5, TPE-CMP can be well dispersed in aqueous solutions at a concentration of 0.1 mg/ml. This also provides a prerequisite for the detection of Tc in water solvents. In addition, we have investigated the fluorescence effect of TPE-CMP in various solvents (Fig. S6). Emission spectra of TPE-CMP in different solvents was presented in Fig. S6a. The inset picture in Fig. S6a shows photograph of TPE-CMP in different solvents, taken under UV illumination. It was confirmed that the fluorescence intensities of TPE-CMP solutions at the same concentration were different for different solvents (Fig. S6b). The fluorescence intensity is the strongest in DMSO solvent, and weakest in aqueous solvent. This phenomenon may be related to solubility on the one hand, and to the polarity of the solution on the other.

It is well known that TPE is a representative AIE chromophore. The rotation of the benzene ring around the TPE molecule can weaken the excitation energy and reduce its luminous intensity in solution [55]. In this experiment, we refer to similar polymers to study the AIE effect of TPE-CMP [56]. The specific experimental procedure was to dissolve TPE-CMP in THF solvent (1 mg/10 mL). Each TPE-CMP solution (1 ml) was placed in ten 10 ml vials and the concentration of water was increased from 0% to 90%. Set the vial aside and sonicate for 10 min to obtain an evenly mixture. However, we found no correlation between fluorescence emission intensity and water concentration (the image was taken under 365 nm excitation) (Fig. S7). The AIE effect of TPE-CMP compared to similar polymers has not been confirmed [56]. This may be due to the structural differences between the two polymers. This phenomenon needs to be further exploration.

Before performing the detection of Tc, we studied the effect of pH on the detection of tetracycline by TPE-CMP (Fig. S8). The pH of the solution had a slight effect on the luminescence of TPE-CMP. The fluorescence intensity of the aqueous TPE-CMP solution was greatest at pH = 6, so we decided to use the pH = 6 solution for further experiments. As shown in Fig. 4 , TPE-CMP solution (0.04 mg/mL in water) displays strong fluorescence emissions centered at 535 nm (c line) by the excitations at 425 nm (a line). With the addition of Tc (100 μg/mL), the intensity of the excitation wavelength (b line) and emission wavelength (d line) are significantly lower than those without Tc, which was further confirmed by the photo taken under UV lamp (365 nm). As can be seen from the inset picture of Fig. 4, it shows the obvious fluorescence quenching after adding Tc. These results indicate that TPE-CMP is feasible as a fluorescent agent for the detection of Tc.

The linearity was examined by recording emission spectra of TPE-CMP after addition of different concentrations of Tc. Fig. 5 a shows that increasing the Tc concentration gradually quenches the fluorescence at 535 nm, but no major change in the maximum emission wavelength is observed. Fig. 5b shows the relationship between fluorescence intensity of TPE-CMP and concentration of Tc, which has a good linear relationship in a wide range from 0 to 100 μg/mL. This linear correlation can be fitted as a function of equation (1):
(1)
y = 822.126–5283.305x (R
2 = 0.990)
where “x” is the mass concentration of Tc and “y” is the fluorescence intensity of TPE-CMP/Tc solution after addition of Tc. The limit of detection (LOD) based on 3σ/S is calculated to be 1.23 μg/mL, where σ is the standard deviation of luminescent intensity of blank for ten times and S is the absolute value of the slope of the regression equation above. It offers advantages over other work in the fluorescence detection of Tc (Table S1).

Selectivity is considered to be a key feature in evaluating the suitability of fluorescent probes for practical applications. To further investigate the effect of potential interfering substances (e.g. potential co-existing ions and antibiotics), fluorescence titration experiments were carried out on the interfering substances under the same experimental conditions and the results are shown in Figs. 6 and 7
. In Fig. 6a, we can see that various metal ions (including Ba2+, Bi3+, Ca2+, Cr6+, Cu2+, K+, Na+, Ni2+, Pb2+) have negligible fluorescence effects on the fluorescence of TPE-CMP, with limited interference with the detection of Tc. As can be seen from Fig. 6b, various anions (including S2O8 2−, S2O5 2−, SO4 2−, CO3 2−, NO3 −, HCO3 −, F−, Cl−, Br−) are unable to cause remarkable interference with the Tc fluorescence detection. In Fig. 7, it is obvious that the fluorescence intensity of TPE-CMP is significantly reduced by the addition of Tc, and the orders of the quenching efficiencies is Tc ≫ ERY > TMP > LVFX > SS > KS > VCH. The detection of Tc is not significantly affected by the co-existence of these antibiotics. These results indicate that TPE-CMP for Tc detection is unaffected by the interfering analytes described above and shows specific recognition of Tc.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The mechanism for detection of Tc
ce:section.ce:para:
In general, the quenching of fluorescent polymers by organic pollutants has been explained by three main mechanisms:(1) Fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) (2) The inner filter effect (IFE) (3) Photoinduced electron transfer (PET). When the emission spectrum of the fluorophore overlaps the absorption spectrum of the acceptor, FRET occurs [57]. It can be seen that overlap between the fluorescence emission spectrum of TPE-CMP and the UV–vis absorption spectrum of Tc can be ignored, which indicates that no fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRTE) has occurred (Fig. S9) [58]. In addition, compared with TPE-CMP solution, Tc solution did not fluoresce at an excitation wavelength of 425 nm (Fig. S10). We further determined the type of fluorescence sensing mechanism by measuring the fluorescence lifetime (Fig. S11). The fluorescence lifetime of TPE-CMP remained constant in the presence of Tc ([Tc] = 100 μg/mL), indicating that quenching mechanism was static rather than dynamic quenching. This also demonstrates that fluorescence quenching is an IFE mechanism and not a FRET mechanism, as FRET has a significantly altered fluorescence lifetime [59]. Apparently, Tc shows strong absorption bands at 275 nm and 355 nm compared to the fluorescence excitation spectrum of TPE-CMP (ex = 425 nm), suggesting that the internal filter effect (IFE) plays almost no role in FL quenching [1,60,61]. Furthermore, The IFE was corrected according to equation (2) and the correction factor of the inner filter effect (IFE) at different concentrations of Tc was calculated as shown in Table S2 [60].
(2)
CF =
F
c o r
F
o b s d
=
2.3 d A e x
1 −
10
− d A e x
×
2.3 s A e m
10
g A e m
1 −
10
− s A e m

where F
obsd and F
cor represent the observed FL intensity of TPE-CMP at 535 nm and the corrected FL intensity by removing IFE from F
obsd and CF is the corrected factor. A
ex and A
em are the absorbance of the detection system at wavelengths of 425 nm and 535 nm, respectively. d represents the cuvette width (1.0 cm); g is the distance from the edge of the excitation beam to the edge of the cuvette (0.4 cm); and the thickness of excitation beam (s) is 0.1 cm. Quenching efficiency come from IFE is less than 1% for Tc as shown in Fig. S12. Therefore, IFE cannot be considered a major factor in fluorescence quenching, which undoubtedly implies the existence of other mechanisms.

In the PET mechanism, electrons are excited from the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) of the TPE-CMP to lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO), and then transferred to the LUMO of Tc [57,62–64]. To verify this, the HOMO and LUMO energy levels of TPE-CMP were calculated by using cyclic voltammetry (CV) studies (Fig. S13). Since the polymer TPE-CMP has a conjugated structure and is rich in electrons, only the oxidation peak is observed in the CV, so the calculated HOMO energy level is −4.68 eV. From the UV–vis absorption spectra (Fig. S14), the optical band gap calculated was 2.62 eV and Thus the LUMO of TPE-CMP is estimated to be −2.06 eV by the empirical formula [65]. Meanwhile, HOMO and LUMO energies of TPE-CMP and Tc antibiotics are also calculated by density functional theory (DFT) calculations (on B3LYP/6-31G∗) (Table S3 and Table S4). The values of HOMO and LUMO for TPE-CMP obtained by electrochemical and solid UV tests do not differ significantly from the results of the DFT calculations. In combination with the calculated energy bands of Tc, this can be used well to explain the PET process, the electron transfer from the LUMO (−2.06 eV) of TPE-CMP to the LUMO (−2.12 eV) of Tc (Fig. 8 , Table S3), The LUMO of TPE-CMP is only 0.06 eV larger than the LUMO of Tc, which favors the photoinduced electron transfer from the LUMO of TPE-CMP to the LUMO orbital of Tc [66]. In addition, we examined the energy orbitals of other antibiotics by DFT calculation. The LUMO orbitals of the other antibiotics were all higher than TPE-CMP Section 1 (Fig. 8, Table S3 and Table S4), which may explain the difficulty of fluorescence quenching of TPE-CMP by other antibiotics. These results confirm that the mechanism for the selective quenching of TPE-CMP fluorescence by Tc is the PET mechanism. These results confirm that the mechanism by which TPE-CMP fluorescence is selectively quenched by Tc is the PET mechanism.

TPE-CMP was used to detect the Tc in real samples. The standard addition method was chosen for detection of Tc in milk and urine samples. concentrations of 10 μg/mL, 50 μg/mL, 100 μg/mL of Tc were added to the treated milk and urine samples and fluorescence response was introduced to detect Tc. The results for milk sample are listed in Table 1 , for urine sample in Table 2 and for wastewater sample in Table 3 . These data show that the Tc detection has good recoveries in real samples (94.96%–112.53% for milk, 96.41%–99.31% for urine, 98.54%–100.52% for wastewater sample). In addition, the detection of tetracycline in those samples had low RSD values. This suggests that TPE-CMP has great potential for application in actual samples.

In brief, a heteroatom-free conjugated tetraphenylethylene polymer (TPE-CMP) based only on carbon and hydrogen atoms was designed and prepared by the Suzuki Coupling reaction. TPE-CMP was successfully used as a fluorescent probe for the effective detection of Tc in aqueous solution. It could achieve the detection of Tc in the concentration range of 0–100 μg/mL with a correlation coefficient of 0.99 and a limit of detection (LOD) of 1.23 μg/mL. In anti-interference experiments with ions and other antibiotics, TPE-CMP showed good selectivity in detecting Tc. In addition, TPE-CMP could be used to detect Tc in milk, urine and wastewater samples with satisfactory recoveries ranging from 94.96% to 112.53%, revealing the feasibility and reliability of TPE-CMP for real sample analysis. TPE-CMP, in this paper, may have a wide range of applications due to its ease of synthesis, sensitive response to Tc and good selectivity. In addition, this heteroatom-free polymer could broaden the range of sensors for detecting antibiotics and provide ideas for the design of fluorescent sensors in the future.

Qiu Zhuang: Investigation, Methodology, characterization. Cheng Zhang: Characterization. Haiyan Zhuang: Theoretical analysis, Review and editing. Hanyu Deng: Characterization. Xiangpeng Lin: Characterization. Yan Li: Characterization. Hao Chen: Characterization. Aming Xie: Conceptualization, Project administration. Wei Dong: Validation, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was financially supported by the Jiangsu Provincial Natural Science Foundation, China (No. BK20180453), National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 11802134), and China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No. 2018M642255).
